topic: Philosophy of Religion: Religious Experience & Language
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Does the verification principle pass its own test?
items.correct: No - the principle itself is neither analytic nor empirically verifiable, a self-referential problem that critics say undermines it.
items.misconception: Verificationism conclusively proved that religious language is meaningless.
items.random_wrong: The verification principle was a method for verifying miracles.
items.why: Students cite Ayer as a knock-down argument without the standard rejoinder that the principle fails its own criterion.

items.q: Did William James think mystical experiences prove God's existence to everyone?
items.correct: No - he held they carry authority for the person who has them and suggest a wider spiritual reality, but they do not compel acceptance by others.
items.misconception: James claimed religious experiences are public proof of God for all rational people.
items.random_wrong: James thought all religious experiences were deliberate frauds.
items.why: James's careful pragmatism is flattened into 'proof'; his actual position distinguishes first-person authority from public evidence.

items.q: Does finding a brain-based trigger debunk a religious experience?
items.correct: Not by itself - explaining how an experience happens does not settle whether its object is real, though such explanations may weaken its evidential force; to assume otherwise risks the genetic fallacy.
items.misconception: If neuroscience can stimulate an experience, religious experiences are thereby proven false.
items.random_wrong: Brain science has shown that no one ever has unusual experiences.
items.why: Cause and content are distinct questions; sophisticated answers weigh physiological evidence without treating it as automatic refutation.

items.q: What are William James's four marks of mystical experience?
items.correct: Ineffability, noetic quality, transiency and passivity
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ineffability, noetic quality, transiency and passivity. “Visions, voices, dreams and trances” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does the verification principle claim?
items.correct: Non-analytic statements are meaningful only if they can be verified empirically
items.misconception: All religious statements are true by definition
items.random_wrong: Meaning depends on the speaker's sincerity
items.why: Correct answer: Non-analytic statements are meaningful only if they can be verified empirically. “All religious statements are true by definition” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was Flew's falsification challenge to religious language?
items.correct: Believers let nothing count against their claims, so the claims die 'the death of a thousand qualifications'
items.misconception: Religious claims are false because science has disproved them
items.random_wrong: Religious language is too emotional to be studied
items.why: Correct answer: Believers let nothing count against their claims, so the claims die 'the death of a thousand qualifications'. “Religious claims are false because science has disproved them” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How did Aquinas think human language applies to God?
items.correct: By analogy, rather than univocally or equivocally
items.misconception: Univocally - words mean exactly the same of God and creatures
items.random_wrong: Equivocally - words about God are completely unrelated to ordinary use
items.why: The answer examiners want is: By analogy, rather than univocally or equivocally. “Univocally - words mean exactly the same of God and creatures” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
